proposals.title: Biophysical Rules Governing Functional Convergence in Protein Kinase Evolution: An Integrated Ancestral Reconstruction and Deep Mutational Scanning Approach
proposals.motivation: Protein kinases are among the most extensively studied enzyme families, yet why distinct evolutionary lineages repeatedly converge on similar catalytic and regulatory phenotypes remains poorly understood at a mechanistic level. Establishing the biophysical constraints that channel convergent evolution has direct implications for understanding drug resistance mutations in cancer kinases and for rational kinase engineering. Prior work has catalogued convergent patterns across kinase phylogenies, but lacks the experimental resolution to distinguish historically contingent from physically inevitable evolutionary outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that functional convergence in kinases is governed by a limited set of biophysical constraints—principally active-site electrostatic geometry and regulatory spine packing—such that distinct ancestral sequence backgrounds can be experimentally driven to converge on equivalent catalytic phenotypes only when these constraints are satisfied, while divergent regulatory mechanisms remain historically contingent and non-convergent.
proposals.method: We will reconstruct ancestral kinase sequences at key phylogenetic nodes spanning tyrosine and serine/threonine kinase families using maximum-likelihood methods (PAML, FireProt-ASR) and synthesize 8-10 resurrected ancestral kinases for biochemical characterization. Each ancestor will undergo comprehensive deep mutational scanning (DMS) using multiplexed activity-based assays in yeast complementation systems to map the complete fitness landscape around the active site and regulatory spine. We will then apply directed evolution on divergent ancestral backgrounds under identical selective pressures for phosphotransfer activity and substrate specificity, tracking whether independent lineages converge on the same sequence solutions. Fitness landscape data will be used to train energy-based predictive models combining Rosetta free-energy calculations with machine learning (ensemble gradient-boosted regressors on structural and sequence features) to define quantitative rules distinguishing convergence-accessible from contingency-dependent functional traits.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first experimentally grounded, predictive framework distinguishing which kinase functional properties are deterministically accessible through convergent evolution from those requiring specific historical sequence contexts. The resulting biophysical rules and predictive models will inform rational approaches to anticipating drug-resistance mutations in oncogenic kinases and will provide general principles applicable to enzyme engineering across protein families.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior phylogenetic analyses that describe convergent patterns post hoc, this approach experimentally tests convergence by subjecting reconstructed ancestral kinases to identical selection and directly measuring whether biophysical constraints force convergent outcomes—bridging evolutionary biology, protein biophysics, and predictive modeling in a framework not previously attempted for any enzyme family.

proposals.title: Dual-Map Kinome Atlas: Decoupling Sequence Phylogeny from Functional Convergence Networks to Reveal Hidden Kinase Relationships
proposals.motivation: The human kinome is conventionally organized by sequence-based phylogenetic trees, yet kinases with low sequence similarity can phosphorylate overlapping substrates, respond to the same inhibitors, and drive identical oncogenic phenotypes. This discordance between evolutionary descent and functional output has led to missed therapeutic opportunities and unexpected off-target drug effects. A systematic framework that separately maps sequence heritage and phenotypic convergence would transform kinase-targeted drug discovery and pathway annotation, particularly in cancers such as glioblastoma where redundant kinase signaling drives therapeutic resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: Kinases that are phylogenetically distant but phenotypically convergent—sharing substrate specificity profiles, downstream pathway activation patterns, and drug sensitivity signatures—form a distinct and identifiable class of functionally analogous enzymes whose relationships cannot be captured by sequence analysis alone, and whose identification will reveal actionable vulnerabilities in tumor signaling networks.
proposals.method: We will compile a multi-omic functional feature matrix for all ~530 human kinases by integrating publicly available phosphoproteomics datasets (PhosphoSitePlus, CPTAC), high-throughput kinase-substrate interaction screens, KINOMEscan drug binding profiles, and CRISPR loss-of-function phenotypic data from DepMap. For each kinase, we construct a functional fingerprint vector encoding substrate motif preferences, pathway enrichment scores (using GSEA on downstream phosphorylation cascades), and drug response similarity computed via Tanimoto coefficients on inhibitor sensitivity profiles. A sequence-based phylogenetic tree is built using standard multiple alignment (MUSCLE) and maximum-likelihood inference (RAxML) on catalytic domain sequences. In parallel, a functional convergence network is constructed by computing pairwise cosine similarity on functional fingerprints and applying community detection (Louvain algorithm) to identify functional kinase clusters. Kinase pairs with high functional similarity (top decile) but low sequence similarity (bottom quartile of phylogenetic distance) are flagged as convergent candidates and validated using structural comparison of active-site conformations via TM-align.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available dual-map kinome atlas that explicitly distinguishes evolutionary relatedness from functional analogy across the entire human kinome. It will nominate specific convergent kinase pairs as candidates for polypharmacology strategies, where a single inhibitor scaffold can target functionally equivalent but sequence-divergent kinases. The atlas will be directly applicable to interpreting kinase inhibitor clinical trial failures and designing rational combination therapies in glioblastoma and other kinase-driven cancers.
proposals.novelty: While kinome trees and individual substrate profiling studies exist, no prior work has systematically constructed and compared a genome-wide functional convergence network against the phylogenetic tree to explicitly identify convergently evolved kinase relationships. The analogy-driven approach of separating descent from typological convergence—borrowed from computational linguistics—provides a conceptual framework that is absent from current kinome classification efforts.

proposals.title: A Functional Kinome Map of Glioblastoma Cellular States Reveals Convergent Kinase Regulation Independent of Phylogeny
proposals.motivation: Kinase function is predominantly inferred from sequence phylogeny, yet this fails to capture functional convergence across divergent clades, especially in complex disease states like cancer. In glioblastoma, kinase-targeted therapies have limited success, partly because our understanding of kinase networks is based on sequence, not their actual role in driving specific oncogenic cellular states (e.g., metabolic reprogramming, immune evasion). An empirical, state-centric map of kinase function is urgently needed to reveal novel, therapeutically actionable regulatory modules.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CRISPR-based perturbation of the kinome, coupled with single-cell state readouts, will reveal significant functional convergence between phylogenetically divergent kinases in regulating core glioblastoma cellular programs, particularly those involving mitochondrial metabolism and immune modulation.
proposals.method: We will perform parallel genome-wide CRISPR-kinase knockout screens in a panel of patient-derived glioblastoma stem cell models and murine immune-competent models. Perturbed cell states will be profiled using high-parameter single-cell technologies: CITE-seq for transcriptional/ surface proteomic state and, for a focused subset, multiplexed phospho-flow cytometry. We will employ a computational pipeline using SCENIC+ for regulatory network inference and a custom graph-based algorithm to cluster kinases by their functional impact profiles across states, explicitly testing for divergence from phylogenetic clustering using distance metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirically derived 'functional kinome' network for glioblastoma, identifying state-specific kinase regulators invisible to sequence analysis. It will directly test the principle of convergent functional evolution in kinase signaling and nominate novel combinatorial kinase targets, particularly at the nexus of mitochondrial function and immune response, relevant to therapies like immune checkpoint blockade.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond correlative phosphoproteomics or sequence-based prediction to causally link each kinase to the regulation of defined, multivariate cellular states. It uniquely integrates CRISPR screening with single-cell multi-omics in immune-competent glioma models to capture kinase function in both tumor-intrinsic and microenvironmental contexts.

proposals.title: Cryo-EM structures of full-length Src-family kinases in lipid nanodiscs reveal membrane-proximal autoinhibition and activation mechanisms
proposals.motivation: Tyrosine kinases are critical drivers in cancer and cellular signaling, but structural understanding is largely limited to isolated catalytic domains due to the challenges of crystallizing full-length, membrane-associated proteins. This gap obscures how regulatory domains and the membrane environment control autoinhibition and activation, which is central to pathologies like glioblastoma and to designing selective inhibitors. The recent success of cryo-EM with lipid nanodiscs for G-protein coupled receptors and ion channels provides a proven, pre-2026 methodological template to overcome this barrier for tyrosine kinases.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reconstituting full-length, myristoylated Src-family kinases (e.g., c-Src, Lyn) into lipid nanodiscs will stabilize native conformations, enabling high-resolution cryo-EM structures that reveal how the SH3, SH2, and catalytic domains are arranged relative to the membrane to enforce autoinhibition and how this is relieved upon activation.
proposals.method: We will express and purify full-length, post-translationally modified human c-Src and Lyn. Proteins will be incorporated into saposin-based lipid nanodiscs with native-like lipid compositions. Cryo-EM grids will be prepared using vitrification robots, and data collected on a 300 keV Titan Krios with a K3 direct electron detector. Single-particle analysis will be performed using RELION-3.1 and cryoSPARC v3, employing 3D classification to separate autoinhibited and active states. Atomic models will be built de novo using Coot and refined with Phenix.real_space_refine.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first high-resolution structures of full-length, membrane-embedded Src-family kinases, defining the structural basis of their regulation. It will establish a generalizable pipeline for studying receptor and non-receptor tyrosine kinases in a near-native environment, providing a structural corpus to inform the rational design of allosteric and isoform-specific cancer therapeutics.
proposals.novelty: While cryo-EM of membrane proteins in nanodiscs is established, its application to solve structures of full-length tyrosine kinases, which are central to cancer biology but notoriously difficult to crystallize, is novel. This directly extends the LARP4 mitochondrial mRNA work by applying advanced structural biology to another class of critical cellular regulators.

proposals.title: CRISPR-State Profiling Links Genetic Regulators to State-Specific Conformational Ensembles of Tyrosine Kinases in Glioblastoma
proposals.motivation: Tyrosine kinases are central drivers of cancer, but their therapeutic targeting is limited by resistance, often due to dynamic shifts in kinase conformational states and signaling networks. Pre-2026, structural biology provides static snapshots, while functional genomics maps regulators, but these fields are disconnected. A unified approach is needed to link the genetic drivers of specific cellular states (e.g., invasive, metabolic, or immune-evasive) to the precise, state-dependent kinase conformations that execute these programs, particularly in recalcitrant cancers like glioblastoma.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genome-wide CRISPR screens coupled with single-cell state readouts will identify distinct genetic modules that drive glioblastoma cells into specific signaling states, and that tyrosine kinases purified from these defined states will exhibit unique, functionally relevant conformational ensembles not observed in bulk or inactive preparations.
proposals.method: We will perform pooled CRISPR knockout screens in patient-derived glioblastoma models, using high-parameter CyTOF and single-cell RNA-seq to classify cells into discrete signaling states (e.g., PI3K-high, IFNγ-responsive). Genetic hits will be validated and used to engineer stable cell lines locked in these states. We will then immuno-purify endogenously tagged tyrosine kinases (e.g., EGFR, MET) from these states and solve their structures using cryo-EM, employing 3D variability analysis and manifold embedding to define state-specific conformational ensembles. Computational integration will map genetic regulators to structural features.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first integrated atlas linking genetic regulators to the dynamic structural biology of tyrosine kinases in defined oncogenic states. It will reveal new allosteric sites and regulatory mechanisms specific to invasive or therapy-resistant cell states, providing a blueprint for designing state-selective kinase inhibitors. The fusion of functional genomics and structural biology will establish a new paradigm for studying signaling dynamics.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in moving beyond static kinase structures by deliberately purifying kinases from genetically defined cellular states, and in directly coupling genome-wide regulator discovery with conformational analysis, a synthesis not previously achieved in pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Reactivation of Ancient Kinase Modules Drives Gliomagenesis
proposals.motivation: Glioblastoma (GBM) remains incurable due to its complex signaling plasticity. While kinase activity is central to GBM, current analyses treat kinases as a flat list, ignoring their deep evolutionary history. Integrating the recently expanded eukaryotic kinome phylogeny with the pseudo-temporal trajectories of neurodevelopment and tumorigenesis offers a novel framework to understand the evolutionary logic of oncogenic signaling dysregulation.
proposals.hypothesis: The transition from normal glial progenitor states to malignant GBM states involves the specific reactivation of signaling modules from phylogenetically ancient kinase clades, which represent core, conserved regulatory nodes.
proposals.method: We will construct a high-resolution phylogenetic tree of the human kinome using pre-2026 databases (e.g., KinBase, Ensembl) and newly sequenced eukaryotic genomes. This tree will be integrated with single-cell RNA-seq data from human fetal brain development and GBM patient samples (pre-2026 public datasets). Using trajectory inference algorithms (Monocle3, Slingshot), we will order cells along developmental and tumorigenic paths. We will then map the expression and inferred activity of kinase families onto these trajectories, correlating phylogenetic age and diversification events with specific transitional cellular states using statistical models.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond cataloging active kinases to reveal the 'deep history' of oncogenic signaling, identifying key phylogenetic clades whose reactivation is causal. It will nominate novel, evolutionarily ancient kinase families as therapeutic targets to disrupt fundamental trajectory nodes in GBM, potentially overcoming adaptive resistance seen with targeting younger, more specific kinases.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in fusing detailed kinome phylogeny with cellular trajectory analysis to test the hypothesis that cancer co-opts ancient, core developmental signaling modules, a concept not addressed by standard differential expression or pathway enrichment studies.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Single-Cell Phosphoproteomic Mapping of Dual-Specificity Kinase Rewiring During Glioblastoma Therapeutic Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Glioblastoma inevitably develops resistance to standard-of-care temozolomide and emerging immunotherapies, yet the signaling dynamics driving this adaptation remain poorly resolved at single-cell resolution. Dual-specificity kinases (DSKs), which phosphorylate both serine/threonine and tyrosine residues, sit at critical signaling crossroads but their substrate rewiring during therapy has never been tracked longitudinally in patient tumors. Understanding how DSK activity shifts across treatment time points could reveal actionable vulnerabilities in the resistant state transitions that defeat current therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that glioblastoma cells undergoing therapy-induced state transitions toward mesenchymal or stem-like resistant phenotypes exhibit systematic rewiring of DSK-substrate networks, particularly involving MAP kinase kinases (MEKs), DYRKs, and CLKs, and that these rewired signaling nodes represent therapeutically targetable dependencies distinct from the treatment-naive kinase landscape.
proposals.method: We will collect longitudinal tumor biopsies from glioblastoma patients at diagnosis, mid-treatment, and recurrence, then perform multiplexed single-cell mass cytometry (CyTOF) using a custom panel of 25-30 phosphosite-specific antibodies targeting known DSK substrates across MAPK, splicing, and cell-cycle pathways. Parallel single-cell RNA sequencing on matched samples will assign transcriptomic cell states (proneural, classical, mesenchymal) to each phosphosignaling profile using weighted nearest-neighbor integration. We will apply kinase-substrate enrichment analysis (KSEA) adapted for single-cell data to infer per-cell DSK activities and construct longitudinal kinase activity trajectories using optimal transport-based trajectory inference (Waddington-OT). Candidate rewired DSK nodes will be functionally validated in patient-derived glioblastoma organoids using selective inhibitors and CRISPR-based kinase-dead knock-ins.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first longitudinal, single-cell-resolution atlas of DSK activity dynamics during glioblastoma treatment, linking specific kinase-substrate network reconfigurations to defined resistant cell states. The resulting dataset will nominate DSK-centered combination therapy strategies designed to block signaling plasticity rather than targeting static oncogenic drivers, offering a new paradigm for overcoming adaptive resistance.
proposals.novelty: While single-cell transcriptomics has catalogued glioblastoma cell states, no study has longitudinally resolved the phosphosignaling logic of state transitions at single-cell level. By focusing specifically on dual-specificity kinases as mechanistic integrators of serine/threonine and tyrosine signaling crosstalk, this work addresses an underexplored kinase family whose therapeutic potential in glioblastoma resistance is largely uncharacterized.

proposals.title: Targeting Allosteric Communication Networks in Receptor Tyrosine Kinases to Achieve Kinome-Wide Selectivity in Glioblastoma
proposals.motivation: Kinase inhibitors targeting the conserved ATP-binding pocket often fail due to lack of selectivity and acquired resistance, limiting their efficacy in cancers like glioblastoma. The PI3K pathway, frequently dysregulated in glioblastoma, exemplifies this therapeutic challenge. A strategy exploiting family-specific regulatory domains and allosteric networks offers a promising orthogonal approach to overcome these limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) relevant to glioblastoma possess family-specific allosteric communication networks, originating from non-catalytic domains, which can be selectively targeted by small molecules to lock kinases in inactive conformations with unprecedented kinome-wide selectivity.
proposals.method: We will integrate long-timescale molecular dynamics simulations using the Anton2 supercomputer to map allosteric pathways in RTKs (e.g., EGFR, MET). Markov State Models will identify cryptic pockets in regulatory domains. Fragment-based screening via NMR and X-ray crystallography will be performed against these distal sites. Hits will be optimized using structure-based design, and selectivity will be validated using kinome-wide profiling (e.g., KINOMEscan) and functional assays in glioblastoma models.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver novel chemical probes that validate allosteric networks as viable drug targets, providing a new strategy for selective kinase modulation. It will establish a computational-experimental pipeline for kinome-wide allosteric targeting, directly addressing the selectivity problem that plagues current ATP-competitive therapies in oncology.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond targeting the kinase domain alone, instead integrating dynamics of full-length RTKs, including their regulatory domains, to discover allosteric sites. This represents a fundamental shift from active-site competition to conformation-selective stabilization via distal, family-specific pockets.

proposals.title: Targeting Kinase Family-Specific Loops with Allosteric Staples: A Structure-Based Strategy for Selective Inhibition in Glioblastoma
proposals.motivation: Kinase inhibitors often suffer from poor selectivity due to the high conservation of the ATP-binding pocket, leading to off-target effects and toxicity. This is a critical problem in cancers like glioblastoma, where dysregulated PI3K and other kinase family signaling drives tumor progression. A new paradigm for achieving high selectivity is urgently needed to develop more effective and safer therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that small-molecule 'staples' or 'clamps' designed to simultaneously engage the conserved catalytic core of a kinase domain and its unique, conformationally diverse peripheral loops (e.g., the P-loop, αC-β4 loop, or activation loop) will confer exceptional family- or isoform-specific inhibition by locking the kinase in an inactive state.
proposals.method: We will integrate computational structural biology and medicinal chemistry. First, we will perform comparative structural analysis of kinase families (e.g., PI3K, AGC, CMGC) using pre-2026 PDB data to identify family-specific loop conformations and dynamics via molecular dynamics simulations. Second, we will use the Rosetta suite for de novo design of 'stapling' compounds that form complementary interactions with both a conserved 'anchor' residue on the kinase scaffold and key residues on a variable loop. Third, top computational hits will be synthesized and tested for binding and inhibition using recombinant kinases, followed by functional assays in patient-derived glioblastoma stem-like cells, assessing impact on proliferation and mitochondrial function (linking to LARP4/mitochondrial mRNA regulation).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel structure-based design framework for kinase inhibitors that prioritizes family-specific loops over the ATP pocket. It will provide proof-of-concept lead compounds with unprecedented selectivity for target kinase families relevant to glioblastoma. Furthermore, it will establish a generalizable 'scaffold-and-loop' targeting strategy applicable to other difficult-to-drug protein families.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel by directly adapting the conceptual framework of antibody engineering (conserved framework, variable CDRs) to small-molecule design for kinases. It moves beyond traditional active-site competition and known allosteric pockets to explicitly target the dynamic, less-conserved structural elements that define kinase family identity.

proposals.title: Convergent Kinase Conformational States as Universal Therapeutic Targets Across Glioblastoma Transcriptional Subtypes
proposals.motivation: Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM) is classified into proneural, classical, and mesenchymal subtypes based on transcriptional signatures resembling neural cell lineages, yet patients show minimal survival benefit from subtype-tailored therapies. This failure suggests that lineage-mimicry markers are epiphenomenal rather than mechanistically actionable. A deeper understanding of the conserved kinase signaling architectures that sustain oncogenic drive across all subtypes could unlock broadly effective, structure-guided therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Despite transcriptional heterogeneity across GBM subtypes, a core set of kinase signaling hubs adopt convergent three-dimensional conformational states that represent the true druggable vulnerabilities, and structure-based inhibitors designed against these shared active-site conformations will suppress tumor growth irrespective of subtype classification.
proposals.method: We will perform quantitative phosphoproteomics (TMT-labeled LC-MS/MS) on patient-derived GBM neurospheres representing each transcriptional subtype to identify kinase-substrate networks that are constitutively active across all subtypes. Convergent kinase nodes—those phosphorylated at equivalent regulatory sites across subtypes—will be prioritized using kinase-substrate enrichment analysis (KSEA) and validated by Western blot with phospho-specific antibodies. For the top three convergent kinases (anticipated candidates include members of the PI3K-AKT-mTOR and RTK-RAS-MAPK axes), we will solve or model active-state crystal structures using X-ray crystallography and AlphaFold2-guided homology modeling, then apply ensemble docking (Glide SP/XP with Schrödinger) and free-energy perturbation (FEP+) to design conformation-selective inhibitors. Lead compounds will be tested in a panel of subtype-representative GBM organoids and orthotopic xenograft models for efficacy, kinome selectivity (KINOMEscan), and blood-brain barrier penetration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic atlas of conformationally convergent kinase states across GBM subtypes, shifting the therapeutic paradigm from transcriptional classification to structural signaling logic. It will deliver validated chemical probes and pre-clinical lead compounds that target the shared oncogenic engine of GBM, offering a rational basis for subtype-agnostic combination therapies.
proposals.novelty: While prior efforts have targeted individual kinases or relied on transcriptomic subtyping to guide therapy selection, no study has systematically mapped the convergent three-dimensional conformational landscape of kinase signaling hubs across GBM subtypes and exploited this structural convergence for conformation-selective drug design—an approach that reframes subtype heterogeneity as pharmacologically irrelevant surface variation atop a unified druggable core.

proposals.title: Structure-Based Design of Selective Kinase Inhibitors Targeting Mitochondrial-Linked Kinase Families in Glioblastoma
proposals.motivation: Kinase inhibitors are crucial cancer therapeutics, but achieving selectivity within conserved catalytic domains remains a major challenge. This is particularly relevant in glioblastoma, where dysregulated kinase signaling and altered mitochondrial metabolism are key drivers of tumor progression and therapy resistance. A structure-based approach to selectively target kinase families involved in mitochondrial function could yield more effective and less toxic treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that leveraging subtle structural variations in the catalytic cores of mitochondrial-linked kinase families (e.g., PIM kinases, PI3K-related kinases) will enable the computational design of highly selective inhibitors that disrupt tumor-specific metabolic and signaling pathways in glioblastoma.
proposals.method: We will integrate AlphaFold2-predicted and existing experimental structures of target kinase domains to perform comparative structural analysis, identifying family-specific conserved and divergent regions. Using Rosetta-based computational protein-ligand docking and free energy perturbation (FEP) calculations, we will design and virtually screen small-molecule libraries for selective binding. Top candidates will be synthesized and tested in glioblastoma cell lines and patient-derived organoids for efficacy, selectivity, and impact on mitochondrial function (e.g., Seahorse analysis).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver novel, selective chemical probes targeting specific kinase families implicated in glioblastoma metabolism. It will provide a structural rationale for kinase inhibitor selectivity and validate a computational pipeline for structure-based drug design applicable to other kinase families. The project will directly test the therapeutic potential of targeting mitochondrial-linked kinases in a lethal cancer.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely integrates the researcher's focus on mitochondrial biology (LARP4, mitochondrial mRNAs) and glioblastoma with a cutting-edge computational structure-based design strategy. It moves beyond targeting single kinases to designing inhibitors for entire, functionally relevant kinase families based on subtle structural features, a non-obvious approach given the historical focus on ATP-competitive pockets.

proposals.title: Iterative DEL-Kinase Panning: A Phage Display-Inspired Platform for Systematic Discovery of Selective Kinase Inhibitors Across the Human Kinome
proposals.motivation: Protein kinases remain among the most important drug targets in oncology, yet achieving selectivity across the 518-member human kinome is a persistent bottleneck. Traditional high-throughput screening campaigns are costly and often yield promiscuous hits with poor selectivity profiles. A method that mimics the iterative enrichment power of phage display but operates in chemical rather than biological space could dramatically accelerate the discovery of selective kinase inhibitors for targets implicated in cancers such as glioblastoma and PI3K-driven tumors.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying iterative cycles of affinity-based selection and PCR amplification to DNA-encoded chemical libraries against purified active kinase domains will enrich for compounds with significantly higher binding affinity and kinase selectivity than single-round screening, enabling systematic profiling across kinase families with fewer resources than conventional HTS.
proposals.method: We will construct a DNA-encoded chemical library (DEL) of approximately 10 million combinatorial small molecules, each covalently linked to a unique DNA barcode, using split-and-pool synthesis on DNA-compatible scaffolds enriched for kinase-privileged pharmacophores. For each target kinase, the recombinant active domain will be immobilized on magnetic beads, and the DEL will be subjected to three to five iterative panning rounds consisting of binding under physiological conditions, stringent washing to remove weak binders, elution of retained compounds, and PCR amplification of their DNA tags to regenerate an enriched sub-library for the next cycle. After final-round sequencing on an Illumina platform, enrichment ratios will be computed per barcode and deconvoluted to chemical structures. Top hits will be resynthesized off-DNA and validated using in vitro kinase activity assays (ADP-Glo), surface plasmon resonance for binding kinetics, and a panel selectivity screen across 50 representative kinases using the DiscoverX scanMAX platform. We will pilot the approach on PI3K isoforms and glioblastoma-relevant receptor tyrosine kinases.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable, cost-effective platform for discovering selective kinase inhibitors by borrowing the iterative enrichment logic of phage display and applying it to encoded chemical libraries. The resulting hit compounds and selectivity data across kinase subfamilies will provide both chemical probes for dissecting kinase signaling in cancer biology and starting points for therapeutic development against difficult-to-drug kinase targets.
proposals.novelty: While DNA-encoded libraries and single-round affinity selections exist independently, the explicit adoption of multi-round iterative panning with PCR-driven amplification cycles—directly modeled on phage display methodology—has not been systematically applied to DEL-based kinase inhibitor discovery. This iterative enrichment strategy is expected to overcome the high false-positive rates of single-pass DEL screens and yield inhibitors with selectivity profiles unattainable by conventional methods.

proposals.title: Defining the Role of LARP4-Mediated Mitochondrial mRNA Stabilization in Non-Mutational PI3K/AKT Pathway Activation in Glioblastoma
proposals.motivation: Kinase pathway activation in cancer is often attributed to genetic mutations, yet many tumors, including glioblastoma (GBM), exhibit pathway hyperactivity without canonical driver mutations. The PI3K/AKT pathway is a key tumor driver, but non-mutational activation mechanisms remain poorly defined. This gap limits therapeutic options, as tumors without mutations may resist targeted inhibitors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the RNA-binding protein LARP4, by stabilizing nuclear-encoded mitochondrial mRNAs and enhancing oxidative phosphorylation, creates a metabolic microenvironment that drives non-mutational, ligand-independent activation of the PI3K/AKT kinase cascade in GBM.
proposals.method: We will use patient-derived GBM models with wild-type PI3K/AKT pathway genes. LARP4 will be knocked down via shRNA, and its impact on mitochondrial function (Seahorse assays) and the phospho-proteome (mass spectrometry) will be assessed. We will employ proximity ligation assays (PLA) and co-immunoprecipitation to test for LARP4-ubiquitin ligase interactions that could stabilize kinase components. In vivo, we will combine LARP4 modulation with anti-CTLA-4 immunotherapy in orthotopic models to test for effects on tumor growth and T-cell function.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify a novel post-transcriptional, metabolism-linked mechanism for constitutive kinase activation, expanding the paradigm beyond genetic and epigenetic causes. It will directly link mitochondrial biology to oncogenic signaling and may reveal LARP4 as a biomarker for PI3K/AKT pathway activity independent of mutation status, suggesting new combination therapies targeting metabolism and immune checkpoints.
proposals.novelty: While LARP4 is known to bind mitochondrial mRNAs, its role in directly promoting oncogenic kinase signaling is unexplored. Connecting mitochondrial mRNA stabilization to non-mutational PI3K/AKT activation via the tumor microenvironment integrates the researcher's expertise in cancer, mitochondria, and ubiquitin in a novel way.

proposals.title: Structural Elucidation of Oncogenic Kinase Mutations in Glioblastoma Reveals Mechanisms of Autoinhibitory Domain Disruption and Altered Ubiquitination Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Oncogenic mutations in kinases often drive cancer by releasing autoinhibitory constraints, leading to constitutive activation, but the precise structural mechanisms for many glioblastoma-relevant kinases remain unclear. Pre-2026 work, including the role of PI3K in tumor-driving pathways, highlights the critical need to understand these conformational changes at atomic resolution to inform next-generation, structure-guided inhibitors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that oncogenic mutations in receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) and PI3K-family members prevalent in glioblastoma disrupt specific intramolecular contacts within autoinhibitory domains, and that these structural changes concomitantly alter kinase interaction surfaces, impacting binding by regulatory proteins like LARP family members or ubiquitin ligases.
proposals.method: We will employ cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) to determine high-resolution structures of full-length, wild-type and mutant kinases (e.g., EGFRvIII, PI3KCA mutants) in complex with relevant regulatory proteins identified from Hunter's recent work on LARP4 and ubiquitin pathways. Computational structural modeling using Rosetta and molecular dynamics simulations (AMBER) will be used to analyze conformational dynamics and predict mutation-induced allosteric networks. Mutational validation will be performed in glioblastoma cell models, assessing kinase activity, protein interactions, and ubiquitination status.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first atomic-level view of how specific glioblastoma-associated kinase mutations mechanically breach autoinhibition, directly linking structure to oncogenic signaling. It will also establish a novel structural framework for understanding how kinase conformation influences non-canonical interactions with RNA-binding proteins (e.g., LARPs) and the ubiquitin machinery, integrating mitochondrial regulation with oncogenic signaling.
proposals.novelty: While kinase structures are known, determining structures of full-length, disease-specific mutants in complex with regulatory partners like LARP proteins is novel. This approach uniquely bridges structural biology with the emerging pre-2026 understanding of kinase roles in broader cellular processes like mitochondrial mRNA regulation.

proposals.title: Systematic RNAi Screening of the Human Kinome to Identify Synthetic Lethal Kinase Dependencies in Glioblastoma Subtypes
proposals.motivation: Glioblastoma remains one of the most treatment-resistant cancers, with median survival under 15 months despite aggressive therapy. While kinase inhibitor trials have largely failed in unselected GBM populations, subtype-specific kinase dependencies likely exist but remain unmapped. A comprehensive RNAi-based functional screen of the human kinome across molecularly defined GBM subtypes could reveal actionable vulnerabilities and guide precision kinase inhibitor strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct glioblastoma molecular subtypes (proneural, classical, mesenchymal) harbor non-overlapping essential kinase dependencies that can be systematically identified through pooled shRNA kinome screens, and that a subset of these dependencies will represent synthetic lethal interactions with common GBM driver mutations in PI3K, PTEN, and EGFR pathways.
proposals.method: We will conduct pooled lentiviral shRNA screens targeting all 518 human kinases using validated libraries (e.g., The RNAi Consortium) across a panel of 12 patient-derived GBM neurosphere lines representing each molecular subtype. Cells will be transduced at low MOI, cultured for 14 population doublings, and barcode abundance quantified by next-generation sequencing. Hit calling will employ MAGeCK and RSA algorithms to identify kinases whose depletion selectively reduces fitness in specific subtypes. Top hits will be validated using orthogonal siRNA pools and CRISPR-Cas9 knockout, with mechanistic follow-up via phosphoproteomics (TMT-labeled LC-MS/MS) to map downstream signaling rewiring. Synthetic lethality with PI3K pathway alterations will be tested by combining kinase knockdown with pharmacological PI3K inhibition.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first comprehensive kinome dependency map stratified by GBM molecular subtype, identifying candidate therapeutic targets for precision oncology approaches. The integration of functional screening with phosphoproteomic profiling will reveal mechanistic explanations for subtype-specific vulnerabilities, potentially nominating rational kinase inhibitor combinations for clinical translation in defined patient populations.
proposals.novelty: While kinome-wide RNAi screens have been applied in breast and lung cancers, no systematic kinome dependency mapping has been performed across molecularly stratified GBM using patient-derived models. The integration of subtype-resolved synthetic lethality analysis with PI3K pathway status—building on Hunter's recent work on PI3K's tumor-driving role—provides a uniquely actionable framework that moves beyond single-target screening toward combinatorial vulnerability discovery.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Phosphoproteomics Reveals PI3K/Akt Activation Heterogeneity and Therapeutic Vulnerability in Glioblastoma
proposals.motivation: PI3K/Akt pathway hyperactivation is a hallmark of glioblastoma, yet bulk tissue analyses mask the cell-to-cell variability in signaling states that drives treatment resistance and tumor recurrence. Understanding how PI3K/Akt activation is distributed across individual tumor cells, immune infiltrates, and stromal populations is critical for designing rational combination therapies. Recent advances in single-cell mass cytometry and multiplexed phospho-protein imaging now make it feasible to map kinase pathway activation at single-cell resolution within the intact tumor microenvironment.
proposals.hypothesis: Glioblastoma tumors harbor discrete single-cell subpopulations defined by distinct PI3K/Akt/mTOR phosphorylation signatures, and these signaling states correlate with differential sensitivity to PI3K inhibitors and CTLA-4 immune checkpoint blockade, such that targeting the resistant phospho-state subpopulation will overcome therapeutic escape.
proposals.method: We will apply mass cytometry (CyTOF) with a 40-marker panel enriched for phosphorylated nodes of the PI3K/Akt/mTOR, MAPK, and JAK/STAT cascades to dissociated glioblastoma specimens from treatment-naive and recurrent patients (n=20 each). Parallel multiplexed ion beam imaging (MIBI) on matched tissue sections will preserve spatial context of phospho-signaling neighborhoods. Single-cell data will be analyzed using FlowSOM clustering and diffusion pseudotime to identify signaling archetypes and state transitions. Patient-derived glioblastoma organoids will then be treated with isoform-selective PI3K inhibitors (alpelisib, inavolisib) alone and combined with anti-CTLA-4 antibody, with CyTOF readouts at 24 and 72 hours to track subpopulation-level pharmacodynamic responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first single-cell atlas of PI3K/Akt phospho-signaling heterogeneity in human glioblastoma, linking signaling states to spatial tumor architecture and immune microenvironment composition. It will identify phospho-signaling biomarkers predictive of PI3K inhibitor resistance and reveal whether combining PI3K inhibition with immune checkpoint blockade eliminates resistant subclones. These findings will directly inform patient stratification strategies for ongoing clinical trials of PI3K-targeted and immunotherapy combinations in glioblastoma.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies characterized PI3K pathway mutations at the genomic level, this proposal uniquely resolves the functional phosphorylation output of these mutations at single-cell and spatial resolution, revealing that genetic homogeneity can mask profound signaling heterogeneity. The integration of phospho-CyTOF with spatial MIBI and functional organoid drug response assays in a unified framework has not been applied to map kinase pathway vulnerability in glioblastoma.

proposals.title: Systematic Mapping of Synthetic Lethal Kinase Pairs for Combinatorial Inhibition of Redundant Oncogenic Signaling in Glioblastoma
proposals.motivation: Single-target kinase inhibitors have largely failed in glioblastoma due to rapid activation of parallel and compensatory signaling pathways, including co-activation of PI3K, MAPK, and receptor tyrosine kinase circuits. Despite decades of kinase biology research pioneered by Hunter and others, rational multi-target strategies remain poorly defined because the combinatorial space of kinase pairs is vast and context-dependent. A systematic approach to identify synthetic lethal kinase combinations could overcome the adaptive resistance that limits current precision oncology in aggressive brain tumors.
proposals.hypothesis: Simultaneous inhibition of computationally predicted synthetic lethal kinase pairs—identified through integration of phosphoproteomics, transcriptomic rewiring signatures, and CRISPR-based combinatorial screening—will produce durable anti-tumor responses in glioblastoma models that are resistant to matched single-agent kinase inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will first construct a glioblastoma-specific kinase dependency network by integrating published phosphoproteomic datasets, TCGA transcriptomic profiles, and protein-protein interaction maps to computationally rank candidate synthetic lethal kinase pairs using a random-walk-with-restart algorithm on a multilayer signaling graph. Top-ranked pairs (approximately 50) will be validated using a pooled dual-guide CRISPR-Cas9 combinatorial knockout screen in patient-derived glioblastoma stem cells spanning all four molecular subtypes. Hits showing synergistic growth suppression (Bliss independence model, combination index < 0.7) will be confirmed with selective small-molecule inhibitor combinations in orthotopic xenograft models, with longitudinal phosphoproteomics to track pathway rewiring and resistance emergence. Throughout, we will monitor mitochondrial function via Seahorse metabolic flux analysis, motivated by emerging evidence linking LARP4-dependent mitochondrial mRNA regulation to tumor cell bioenergetics.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated catalog of synthetic lethal kinase pairs specific to glioblastoma subtypes, providing a rational basis for designing combination clinical trials. It will also yield a generalizable computational-experimental pipeline for mapping kinase redundancy networks in other cancers, moving the field beyond empirical doublet selection toward mechanistically grounded combinatorial strategies.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior combinatorial screens that test arbitrary kinase pairs, our approach integrates multilayer network inference with functional CRISPR validation to prioritize pairs based on predicted signaling redundancy rather than empirical guessing, and uniquely incorporates mitochondrial metabolic vulnerability as a selection criterion for combination efficacy.

proposals.title: Systematic identification of non-canonical lipid kinase activities in the human kinome and their role in glioblastoma mitochondrial metabolism
proposals.motivation: The human kinome is canonically defined by protein phosphorylation, yet atypical activities, such as lipid phosphorylation by PI3K-family members, drive oncogenic signaling. In glioblastoma, metabolic reprogramming and mitochondrial function are critical for tumor progression, but the potential contribution of mislocalized or repurposed 'protein' kinases with lipid kinase activity to these processes is unexplored. This gap limits our understanding of metabolic drivers and potential therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of cataloged 'protein' kinases localize to mitochondria and possess non-canonical lipid kinase activities that regulate mitochondrial phospholipid metabolism, thereby supporting glioblastoma cell survival and growth.
proposals.method: We will perform an in vitro kinase screen of a curated panel of understudied human protein kinases against a diverse lipid library, using quantitative mass spectrometry. Hits will be validated in glioblastoma cell lines via CRISPRi knockdown and complemented with wild-type or catalytic-dead mutants. Proximity-dependent biotinylation (BioID) and subcellular fractionation coupled with proteomics will identify mitochondrial interactors and localization. Functional impact on mitochondrial respiration, membrane potential, and phospholipid composition will be assessed using Seahorse analysis and lipidomics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel link between non-canonical kinase activities, mitochondrial lipid metabolism, and glioblastoma pathobiology. It will generate a resource of kinome lipid kinase activities and identify new metabolic vulnerabilities that could be targeted therapeutically, moving beyond the traditional protein-centric view of kinase signaling.
proposals.novelty: While PI3K's lipid kinase role is known, systematically screening the 'protein' kinome for mitochondrial lipid kinases is novel. Integrating this with glioblastoma mitochondrial metabolism directly bridges Tony Hunter's expertise in kinases and cancer with recent themes of mitochondrial function and gliobiology.

proposals.title: Systematic Characterization of 71 Novel Kinases: Substrate Identification, Tissue-Specific Expression, and Functional Roles in Glioblastoma Metabolism
proposals.motivation: The kinome is a major driver of cellular signaling, yet a significant fraction of protein kinases remains uncharacterized, representing a critical gap in our understanding of signaling networks in health and disease. Given the established roles of kinases in cancer, particularly in glioblastoma (GBM) where pathways like PI3K are drivers, these novel kinases may harbor unexplored oncogenic or tumor-suppressive functions, potentially linked to metabolic and mitochondrial regulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of the 71 novel kinases phosphorylates key substrates involved in mitochondrial function and RNA metabolism, and that their dysregulated expression contributes to glioblastoma pathogenesis by altering tumor cell metabolism and immune interactions.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged approach: 1) Use analog-sensitive kinase allele technology (ASKA) in HEK293T and patient-derived GBM cells for in vitro substrate identification via quantitative phosphoproteomics (LC-MS/MS). 2) Determine tissue and tumor expression using RNA-seq from TCGA/GTEx databases and validate with immunohistochemistry on GBM tissue microarrays. 3) Functionally characterize top candidates via CRISPRi knockdown in GBM models, assessing impacts on mitochondrial respiration (Seahorse), proliferation, and response to T cell-derived IFNγ, linking to the LARP4-mitochondrial mRNA axis.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first functional map for these 71 kinases, directly linking novel signaling nodes to mitochondrial biology and GBM pathophysiology. It will identify new potential therapeutic targets and provide a public resource of kinase-substrate relationships for the community.
proposals.novelty: While kinase discovery is ongoing, this proposal uniquely integrates systematic substrate mapping with a focused investigation into their roles in GBM and mitochondrial metabolism, bridging the gap between kinome annotation and the specific research themes of the Hunter lab.

proposals.title: Resurrection of Kinase Pseudogenes: Systematic Identification of Regulatory and Reactivatable Elements within the Human Kinase Pseudogenome
proposals.motivation: The human genome harbors 106 annotated kinase pseudogenes, long dismissed as evolutionary relics. However, emerging evidence shows that pseudogenes can act as ceRNA (competing endogenous RNA) sponges, produce antisense transcripts, and regulate their parental kinases—functions with direct relevance to cancer signaling. Given Tony Hunter's foundational work on tyrosine kinase signaling and cancer biology, determining which kinase pseudogenes retain functional regulatory capacity could reveal an overlooked layer of kinome regulation.
proposals.hypothesis: A subset of the 106 human kinase pseudogenes are transcriptionally active in cancer contexts and function as cis- or trans-regulators of parental kinase expression, with select pseudogenes harboring restorable open reading frames capable of producing truncated proteins with dominant-negative or neomorphic signaling activity.
proposals.method: We will perform a three-phase investigation. First, we will systematically profile transcription of all 106 kinase pseudogenes across TCGA pan-cancer RNA-seq datasets and matched GTEx normal tissues, identifying those with cancer-specific or tissue-specific expression using DESeq2 and weighted gene co-expression network analysis (WGCNA). Second, for transcribed pseudogenes, we will computationally predict ceRNA interactions with parental kinases using miRanda and TargetScan, validate candidates via luciferase reporter assays and AGO2-RIP-seq in glioblastoma and breast cancer cell lines, and assess regulatory impact through antisense oligonucleotide knockdown followed by phosphoproteomics. Third, we will identify pseudogenes with near-intact ORFs, engineer minimal codon repairs via CRISPR base editing, express the resurrected proteins in kinase-null backgrounds, and assay catalytic activity using in vitro kinase assays and substrate phosphorylation profiling.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive functional atlas of human kinase pseudogenes, distinguishing regulatory from inert loci and identifying candidates for therapeutic targeting. It will establish a general framework for pseudogene resurrection applicable beyond kinases and may uncover novel dominant-negative regulators of oncogenic kinase pathways relevant to glioblastoma and other cancers.
proposals.novelty: While individual pseudogene regulatory functions have been reported, no systematic effort has addressed the entire kinase pseudogenome. The combination of ceRNA network analysis with CRISPR-mediated ORF resurrection to test both regulatory and coding potential of pseudogenes is unprecedented and bridges computational genomics with synthetic biology in a clinically relevant kinase signaling context.

proposals.title: A Comprehensive Functional and Spatial Catalog of the Human Phosphatome Reveals Novel Glioblastoma-Associated Regulatory Hubs
proposals.motivation: While the human kinome has been systematically characterized, the complementary phosphatase network remains fragmented, creating a critical gap in understanding phospho-signaling homeostasis. In cancer, especially glioblastoma, dysregulated phosphatases are emerging as key tumor suppressors or drivers, but a unified resource linking phosphatase identity, subcellular localization, and substrate networks is lacking. This limits our ability to target these enzymes therapeutically, akin to kinase inhibitors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, multi-omics catalog of the human phosphatome will reveal novel, compartment-specific phosphatase complexes—particularly in the nucleus and mitochondria—that are dysregulated in glioblastoma, identifying new regulatory hubs for therapeutic intervention.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-tiered approach: 1) Bioinformatic curation and phylogenetic analysis to define the core human phosphatome, building on pre-2026 databases like PhosphataseWiki. 2) Endogenous tagging (e.g., APEX2) of phosphatases in glioblastoma cell lines for proximity-dependent biotinylation (BioID) to map protein-protein interaction networks, with a focus on nuclear (e.g., PP1) and mitochondrial-associated phosphatases. 3) Integration with ubiquitin-proteomics data to identify phosphatase stability regulators, and functional validation using CRISPRi screens for phosphatase genes essential for glioblastoma cell survival.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first integrated, functional map of the human phosphatome, providing a public resource analogous to the kinome. It will directly link specific phosphatases and their complexes to glioblastoma pathophysiology, identifying novel tumor dependencies and potential drug targets. The data will also illuminate phosphatase roles in organelle-specific signaling, connecting to mitochondrial function and nuclear regulation.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond simple genomic cataloging to create a spatially and functionally resolved network map of phosphatases, integrating their interactors, regulators, and cancer-specific essentiality. It uniquely leverages Dr. Hunter's expertise in glioblastoma, mitochondrial biology (via LARP4), and ubiquitin signaling to dissect phosphatase biology in a disease-relevant context.

proposals.title: Tissue-Specific Kinome Profiling Reveals Distinct Active Kinase Networks in Glioblastoma Subtypes and Associated Microglia
proposals.motivation: Kinase activity drives oncogenic signaling, but bulk tumor analyses obscure cell-type-specific signaling networks. In glioblastoma (GBM), the tumor microenvironment, including microglia, critically influences progression and therapy response. Pre-2026, there is no comprehensive map of the *active* kinome within specific cell types of human tissues, limiting our understanding of context-specific signaling drivers and therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, cell-type-specific active kinase networks exist within GBM tumors and their microenvironment, and that profiling these networks will reveal novel, therapeutically targetable signaling dependencies not apparent from bulk analyses.
proposals.method: We will perform active kinome profiling using multiplexed inhibitor bead (MIB) / mass spectrometry (MS) on fluorescence-activated cell-sorted (FACS) populations from primary human GBM samples: tumor cells (EGFR+/SOX2+) and tumor-associated microglia (TMEM119+/P2RY12+). Kinase activity will be inferred from binding to immobilized, broad-spectrum kinase inhibitors (e.g., VI16832, purvalanol B). We will integrate this with RNA-seq data to distinguish expressed from catalytically active kinases. Computational analysis will use the KSEA (Kinase-Substrate Enrichment Analysis) algorithm and network modeling to identify cell-type-specific active kinase modules.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first high-resolution map of cell-type-specific kinase activity in GBM, directly linking to the researcher's interest in tumor and mitochondrial (via LARP4/mitochondrial mRNA regulation) biology. It will identify novel kinase targets for cell-specific therapeutic intervention and provide a framework for understanding signaling crosstalk in the tumor microenvironment.
proposals.novelty: While kinome profiling exists, applying it to FACS-purified primary human tissue cell types to deconvolute tissue-level signaling is novel. This approach moves beyond bulk tumor or cell line analyses to reveal the true signaling architecture of complex tissues.

proposals.title: Redundant GRB2 Recruitment to Focal Adhesion Kinase via Alternative Phosphotyrosine Sites and Non-Canonical Interactions in Glioblastoma Invasion
proposals.motivation: Focal Adhesion Kinase (FAK) signaling, particularly through its Tyr925-GRB2-SOS-RAS axis, is a critical driver of glioblastoma (GBM) cell invasion and tumor progression. However, clinical targeting of FAK kinase activity has shown limited efficacy, suggesting compensatory signaling pathways. Understanding alternative, redundant mechanisms for GRB2 recruitment to FAK could reveal novel, therapeutically targetable nodes in GBM. This aligns with the growing recognition of pathway redundancy in cancer cell signaling and resistance mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in the absence of FAK pTyr925, GRB2 is recruited to FAK through alternative phosphorylated tyrosine residues (e.g., Tyr397, Tyr576/577) or via non-SH2 domain interactions, such as binding to FAK-associated ubiquitinated proteins or RNA-binding proteins, to sustain pro-invasive RAS/MAPK signaling in glioblastoma cells.
proposals.method: We will employ isogenic GBM cell lines (U87, patient-derived models) expressing FAK Y925F mutants. Proximity-dependent biotinylation (BioID) with GRB2 as bait will identify interacting partners in wild-type vs. mutant contexts. Phosphoproteomic analysis (LC-MS/MS) of immunoprecipitated FAK complexes will map alternative phospho-tyrosine sites. Interactions will be validated by co-IP and PLA. Functional redundancy will be assessed by measuring invasion (Boyden chamber), RAS-GTP activation, and ERK phosphorylation upon combinatorial inhibition of FAK (defactinib) and candidate redundant pathways (e.g., ubiquitination or mitochondrial function via LARP4 modulation, given its role in mRNA regulation).
proposals.contribution: This work will define the molecular landscape of redundant GRB2-FAK interactions, moving beyond the canonical pTyr925 paradigm. It will identify new potential co-targets for FAK inhibition in GBM. Furthermore, it will bridge concepts of adhesion signaling with RNA metabolism and ubiquitination, areas of emerging interest in cancer cell biology.
proposals.novelty: While Tyr925 is the established GRB2 binding site, the explicit testing for in vivo redundancy via other FAK phosphosites or non-SH2 mechanisms in a therapeutically relevant GBM model is novel. Integrating concepts from the researcher's recent work on LARP4 (RNA-binding/mitochondria) and ubiquitin suggests an innovative exploration of alternative recruitment scaffolds beyond direct phospho-tyrosine binding.

proposals.title: Identification of Src-family kinase-independent pathways for FAK Tyr925 phosphorylation and GRB2 recruitment in glioblastoma and other cancer cell types
proposals.motivation: Focal Adhesion Kinase (FAK) signaling, particularly at Tyr925 leading to GRB2/SOS recruitment and RAS/MAPK activation, is a critical driver of tumor progression and invasion. The canonical model posits that Src-family kinases (SFKs) are essential for this phosphorylation event. However, emerging evidence suggests kinase signaling redundancy in cancer, and our preliminary data hint that FAK signaling may persist in certain contexts despite SFK inhibition, potentially explaining therapeutic resistance in tumors like glioblastoma.
proposals.hypothesis: In specific cancer cell types, including glioblastoma, FAK phosphorylation at Tyr925 and subsequent GRB2 recruitment can be mediated by kinases other than Src-family members, such as receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) or other cytoplasmic tyrosine kinases, providing an alternative route for pro-tumorigenic signaling.
proposals.method: We will employ a panel of glioblastoma and other cancer cell lines with varying SFK dependency. Using CRISPR/Cas9 to generate FAK-null lines, we will reconstitute them with wild-type or Y925F mutant FAK. We will assess Tyr925 phosphorylation dynamics via phospho-specific flow cytometry and western blot following pharmacological SFK inhibition (e.g., dasatinib) and/or RTK inhibition. Proximity ligation assays (PLA) will quantify FAK-GRB2 interaction. Candidate alternative kinases will be identified through co-immunoprecipitation-mass spectrometry (co-IP-MS) of FAK complexes under SFK-inhibited conditions and validated via siRNA knockdown.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the canonical SFK-centric view of FAK activation by mapping alternative kinase pathways. It will provide a mechanistic basis for understanding intrinsic resistance to SFK/FAK-targeted therapies in solid tumors and identify novel kinase targets for combinatorial treatment strategies, particularly in recalcitrant cancers like glioblastoma.
proposals.novelty: While SFK's role in FAK activation is well-established, the systematic investigation of compensatory, SFK-independent pathways for the specific GRB2-docking site Tyr925 in defined cancer cell types is unexplored and could reveal a critical signaling bypass mechanism.

proposals.title: In Vivo Mapping of the FAK-GRB2-Sos-Ras Signaling Axis in Glioblastoma: A Direct Role for FAK pTyr925 in Ras Activation
proposals.motivation: Focal Adhesion Kinase (FAK) is a critical driver of glioblastoma (GBM) progression, promoting invasion and survival. While FAK phosphorylation at Tyr925 creates a binding site for the GRB2-Sos adaptor complex, a canonical activator of Ras, it remains unclear if this specific phosphorylation event directly activates Ras signaling in the complex in vivo environment of a tumor cell. Elucidating this direct link is crucial, as hyperactive Ras signaling is a hallmark of cancer and intersects with pathways like PI3K, a known tumor driver.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that FAK phosphorylation at Tyr925 is necessary and sufficient for the direct recruitment of the GRB2-Sos complex in intact GBM cells, leading to the localized activation of Ras at focal adhesions.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of FRET-based Ras biosensors (Raichu-Ras) and fluorescence lifetime imaging (FLIM) to quantify spatiotemporal Ras activation in live patient-derived GBM cells. This will be coupled with the expression of well-characterized, pre-2026 dominant-negative mutants (e.g., FAK Y925F, GRB2 SH2 domain mutant R86K) and wild-type controls. Co-immunoprecipitation and proximity ligation assays (PLA) will validate complex formation. Experiments will be conducted under conditions mimicking tumor microenvironment stresses relevant to the researcher's interests, such as nutrient deprivation impacting mitochondrial function.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map a key in vivo signaling connection between FAK and Ras in GBM, providing mechanistic insight into how integrin and growth factor signaling converge. It will establish whether FAK pTyr925 is a bona fide direct activator of oncogenic Ras, informing the rationale for combination therapies targeting FAK and downstream Ras effectors.
proposals.novelty: While the FAK-GRB2 interaction is known, directly demonstrating its functional consequence—specific Ras activation in living tumor cells—using precise biosensors and dominant-negative constructs within a pathologically relevant GBM model is novel. This moves beyond correlative biochemistry to causal, spatially resolved signaling analysis.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Kinase Hierarchy at FAK Tyr925: Defining the Roles of c-Src and Alternative Tyrosine Kinases in Integrin-Dependent Phosphorylation
proposals.motivation: Phosphorylation of focal adhesion kinase (FAK) at Tyr925 creates a Grb2-binding site that couples integrin engagement to Ras-MAPK signaling, governing cell migration, survival, and metastatic potential. Although c-Src is widely presumed to be the principal Tyr925 kinase, direct biochemical proof is incomplete, and Src-family kinase (SFK) inhibitors do not fully abolish pY925 in several cell types, suggesting additional or compensatory kinases may contribute. Resolving the kinase hierarchy at this site is essential for rational therapeutic targeting of integrin-FAK signaling in cancer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that c-Src is the primary but not sole kinase responsible for FAK Tyr925 phosphorylation upon integrin ligation, and that at least one additional SFK member or a non-SFK tyrosine kinase compensates when c-Src activity is ablated, maintaining Grb2-MAPK output in adherent cells.
proposals.method: We will employ CRISPR-Cas9 knockout panels in isogenic MEF and glioblastoma cell lines to individually and combinatorially delete c-Src, Fyn, and Yes, then assess pY925 levels by quantitative phospho-specific immunoblotting and targeted mass spectrometry following fibronectin-induced integrin clustering. An analog-sensitive (AS) allele strategy will be used to achieve acute, selective kinase inhibition with bulky PP1 analogs, distinguishing direct phosphorylation from scaffold-dependent effects. In vitro kinase assays using purified recombinant kinase domains and a FAK peptide substrate spanning residues 920-930 will determine catalytic efficiency (kcat/Km) for each candidate. Finally, proximity ligation assays and BioID proximity labeling at endogenous FAK will map which kinases physically associate with FAK at focal adhesions within minutes of integrin engagement.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a definitive kinase-substrate assignment for FAK pY925 under physiological integrin signaling, resolving a long-standing ambiguity in adhesion biology. By identifying compensatory kinases, the study will inform combination strategies for SFK inhibitor therapies in glioblastoma and other FAK-dependent cancers, and provide a validated phosphoproteomic framework for mapping kinase redundancy at individual tyrosine sites.
proposals.novelty: Prior studies relied primarily on pharmacological SFK inhibitors or dominant-negative constructs that lack isoform specificity and cannot exclude scaffolding roles. Our combinatorial genetic ablation paired with analog-sensitive alleles and quantitative mass spectrometry will, for the first time, deconvolve the individual catalytic contributions of each candidate kinase at a single FAK tyrosine residue in living cells.

proposals.title: Systematic Identification of SH2-Domain Proteins Recruited to Phosphorylated Focal Adhesion Kinase (FAK) in Glioblastoma Using Yeast Two-Hybrid Screening
proposals.motivation: Focal Adhesion Kinase (FAK) is a critical signaling hub in glioblastoma, promoting tumor invasion and survival through its phosphorylated tyrosine residues, which serve as docking sites for SH2-domain proteins. While GRB2 and Src are known interactors, the full repertoire of SH2-domain proteins recruited to active FAK remains poorly characterized, limiting our understanding of its pro-tumorigenic signaling networks. This gap is significant given the therapeutic interest in FAK inhibition and the role of ubiquitin-mediated protein degradation in regulating such interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that phosphorylated FAK recruits a broader network of SH2-domain-containing proteins beyond GRB2 and Src, including novel interactors that link FAK signaling to mitochondrial function and protein turnover pathways relevant to glioblastoma progression.
proposals.method: We will perform a high-throughput yeast two-hybrid (Y2H) screen using the phosphorylated FAK C-terminal domain (containing key tyrosines Y576, Y577, Y861, and Y925) as bait against a human SH2-domain library. Positive interactions will be validated through co-immunoprecipitation and Western blotting in glioblastoma cell lines. Candidate interactors will be prioritized based on their known roles in cancer, ubiquitination (linking to Hunter's recent work), or mitochondrial regulation (informed by Hunter's interest in LARP4 and mitochondrial mRNA binding).
proposals.contribution: This work will map a comprehensive interactome for phosphorylated FAK, identifying novel signaling partners that could explain FAK's diverse roles in glioblastoma. It will provide a resource for understanding how FAK integrates adhesion, growth, and metabolic signals, potentially revealing new therapeutic targets within the FAK signaling axis.
proposals.novelty: While Y2H screens for FAK interactors have been done, a focused screen targeting the SH2-domain proteome with a phospho-mimetic FAK bait in the context of glioblastoma is novel. This approach directly integrates Hunter's expertise in glioblastoma, ubiquitin, and mitochondrial biology to uncover non-canonical FAK interactions.

proposals.title: Identification of Protein Tyrosine Phosphatases Responsible for FAK Tyr925 Dephosphorylation and Termination of Integrin-MAPK Signaling
proposals.motivation: Focal adhesion kinase (FAK) phosphorylation at Tyr925 creates a Grb2 binding site that activates RAS-MAPK signaling downstream of integrin engagement, driving cell migration, survival, and tumor invasion. While the kinases and adaptors that activate this pathway are well characterized, the phosphatases that dephosphorylate FAK Tyr925 to terminate signaling remain largely unknown. Understanding this negative regulatory mechanism is critical because its dysregulation could explain sustained MAPK activation in cancers, particularly glioblastoma and other aggressive tumors where FAK is constitutively hyperphosphorylated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific protein tyrosine phosphatases, most likely members of the PTP1B, SHP2, or PTP-PEST families, selectively dephosphorylate FAK at Tyr925 to terminate integrin-coupled MAPK signaling, and that loss of these phosphatases sustains aberrant FAK-MAPK activation in cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will employ a substrate-trapping mutant approach using catalytically inactive (D-to-A) mutants of candidate PTPs (PTP1B, SHP2, PTP-PEST, PTPN12, and DEP-1) expressed in HEK293T and U87 glioblastoma cells to capture phospho-FAK Tyr925 complexes, followed by co-immunoprecipitation and mass spectrometry validation. Candidate phosphatases will be individually depleted by siRNA and CRISPR-Cas9 knockout, with phospho-specific immunoblotting and quantitative phosphoproteomics used to measure Tyr925 phosphorylation kinetics after integrin stimulation on fibronectin-coated substrates. In vitro dephosphorylation assays using recombinant PTP catalytic domains and synthetic phospho-Tyr925 peptides will establish direct enzyme-substrate relationships and determine kinetic parameters (Km, kcat). Finally, we will assess the functional consequences of phosphatase depletion on downstream ERK1/2 activation, cell migration (transwell and wound-healing assays), and anchorage-independent growth in glioblastoma cell models.
proposals.contribution: This study will identify the specific phosphatase or phosphatases that serve as the off-switch for FAK Tyr925-MAPK signaling, filling a major gap in integrin signaling biology. The findings will reveal whether phosphatase loss contributes to constitutive FAK-MAPK activation in glioblastoma and other cancers, potentially uncovering new therapeutic vulnerabilities. Additionally, defining this regulatory circuit will provide a framework for understanding how signal duration at focal adhesions is controlled.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has focused on phosphatases that regulate FAK Tyr397 autophosphorylation and overall FAK activity, no systematic study has identified the phosphatases specific to the Tyr925-Grb2-MAPK axis. The combination of substrate-trapping proteomics with site-specific phosphorylation kinetics in glioblastoma models provides a uniquely rigorous approach to dissect this understudied signal termination mechanism.

proposals.title: Dissecting Causation from Correlation: Systematic Evaluation of Tyrosine Phosphorylation as a Passenger Event in Oncogenic Transformation
proposals.motivation: Since the discovery of v-Src as a tyrosine kinase in 1980, elevated tyrosine phosphorylation has been assumed to be a causal driver of malignant transformation. However, many tyrosine kinase inhibitors show limited clinical efficacy beyond specific fusion-driven cancers, and phosphoproteomic studies reveal that the majority of transformation-associated phosphotyrosine sites have no validated functional role. Rigorously distinguishing causal from coincidental tyrosine phosphorylation events is critical for rational therapeutic targeting and for correcting a foundational assumption in cancer biology.
proposals.hypothesis: A substantial fraction of tyrosine phosphorylation events observed during oncogene-driven transformation are passenger modifications that are dispensable for the transformed phenotype, and transformation instead depends on a minimal subset of phosphotyrosine-dependent signaling nodes acting in concert with serine/threonine and non-kinase mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR base editing to systematically mutate individual tyrosine residues (Tyr-to-Phe) at the top 200 transformation-associated phosphotyrosine sites identified by quantitative phosphoproteomics in isogenic cell lines expressing activated Src, EGFR, or BCR-ABL. Transformation readouts—anchorage-independent growth, proliferation, invasion, and xenograft tumor formation—will be scored for each mutant to classify each site as essential, contributory, or dispensable. Combinatorial mutant libraries will be constructed using multiplexed base editing to define the minimal phosphotyrosine signature required for transformation. Parallel RNA-seq and interactome mapping via proximity labeling (TurboID) will identify compensatory serine/threonine kinase or ubiquitin-dependent pathways that sustain transformation when tyrosine phosphorylation is ablated.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first systematic, loss-of-function catalog distinguishing driver from passenger tyrosine phosphorylation events in transformation, directly informing which phosphotyrosine nodes merit therapeutic investment. It will reveal compensatory non-tyrosine signaling mechanisms that maintain the transformed state, opening new combination therapy strategies. The dataset will serve as a public resource for the cancer signaling community.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior gain-of-function or inhibitor-based studies that assume tyrosine phosphorylation is causal, this approach uses precision base editing to test necessity at single-residue resolution across multiple oncogenes simultaneously, directly challenging a 45-year-old paradigm from within the laboratory that originally established it.

proposals.title: Identification and Functional Characterization of a 50kDa Regulatory Protein That Controls pp60src Kinase Activity
proposals.motivation: The pp60src tyrosine kinase is a critical oncogenic driver, yet the upstream mechanisms controlling its catalytic activity remain incompletely understood. A 50kDa protein that coprecipitates with pp60src has been observed in multiple studies but dismissed as a passive substrate. Determining whether this protein instead functions as an active regulator of Src could reveal a new layer of kinase control relevant to cancer signaling and therapeutic targeting.
proposals.hypothesis: The 50kDa coprecipitating protein is not merely a substrate of pp60src but functions as a direct allosteric or scaffolding regulator that modulates Src kinase activity, and its depletion or mutation will alter pp60src-dependent signaling and transformation.
proposals.method: We will first identify the 50kDa protein using immunoprecipitation of pp60src from v-Src-transformed fibroblasts followed by mass spectrometry. Candidate interactors will be validated by reciprocal co-immunoprecipitation, GST pulldown with purified recombinant domains, and proximity ligation assays. To test regulatory function, we will perform in vitro kinase assays measuring pp60src autophosphorylation (Y416) and inhibitory phosphorylation (Y527) in the presence and absence of recombinant 50kDa protein, and use CRISPR-Cas9 knockout and siRNA knockdown in Src-transformed cell lines to assess effects on Src activity, downstream ERK/STAT3 phosphorylation, cell proliferation, and anchorage-independent growth. Domain mapping via truncation and point mutants will localize the interaction interface and distinguish allosteric regulation from competitive substrate behavior.
