ideas.title: Atmospheric regime atlas approach to pan-cancer cellular state plasticity
ideas.description: This approach draws an analogy between mapping cellular state plasticity across cancer types and the construction of global atmospheric circulation regime atlases in climate science. Climate scientists faced a structurally similar challenge: identifying which dynamical patterns are universal across geographic regions versus locally driven by topography and ocean currents. They solved this by projecting high-dimensional atmospheric fields into a shared low-dimensional regime space using clustering methods (self-organizing maps, Gaussian mixture models), then systematically quantifying how much variance in each region was explained by universal modes versus local residuals. We adapt this framework by treating each cancer type as a geographic region and cellular transcriptomic states as the atmospheric field. First, single-cell RNA-seq data from multiple cancer types are projected into a shared plasticity state space using the four-state framework (mesenchymal, proliferative, inflammatory, neural-crest-like or equivalent archetypes). Second, a decomposition separates universal plasticity programs—state transitions that recur across cancers regardless of tissue of origin—from tissue-specific programs shaped by the local microenvironment and lineage constraints. Third, borrowing the climate science practice of cross-basin model transfer, transition models learned from data-rich tumor types are applied to predict plasticity dynamics in rarer cancers where longitudinal or perturbation data are scarce. This yields a pan-cancer plasticity atlas that not only catalogs states but quantifies the universal versus context-dependent nature of each transition, identifies shared therapeutic vulnerabilities in universal programs, and flags tissue-specific escape routes that require tailored intervention strategies.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Profile HMMs with Contextual Emission States for Kinase Functional Classification
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from historical linguistics, which uses phylogenetic models incorporating semantic and syntactic context to trace word evolution across languages, we adapt this solution to kinase classification. We propose building a phylogenetic profile Hidden Markov Model where emission states represent short, conserved functional motifs and their structural contexts, and hidden states represent kinase functional or subfamily classes. This model, trained on a phylogenetically diverse set of kinase domains, will sensitively classify divergent sequences by detecting conserved patterns of functional 'grammar'—the specific arrangement and co-evolution of key motifs—rather than relying on global sequence similarity, thereby improving the detection and functional prediction of evolutionarily distant kinases.

ideas.title: Borrowing microbial community assembly frameworks to engineer and track multi-state heterogeneity in tumor organoids
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between tumor cell-state heterogeneity in organoids and phenotypic heterogeneity in synthetic microbial communities. In microbial ecology, defined consortia are assembled in controlled microenvironments (droplets, chemostats, microfluidic chambers) and tracked using fluorescent transcriptional reporters to understand how multiple coexisting phenotypic states—persisters, biofilm formers, motile cells, sporulators—emerge, stabilize, and interconvert under environmental pressure. Dynamical systems frameworks (energy landscapes, attractor basin analysis) are then applied to map transition probabilities between states. Adapting this approach, we propose engineering patient-derived tumor organoids with a multiplexed lentiviral reporter system in which each of the four recognized cancer cell states (mesenchymal-like, proliferative, inflammatory, and quiescent/stem-like) is tagged with a distinct fluorescent protein driven by state-specific promoters. Organoids would be cultured in millifluidic devices permitting precise control over oxygen gradients, growth factor concentrations, and stromal co-culture conditions—analogous to the environmental control used in synthetic ecology. Longitudinal live imaging and single-cell RNA sequencing at defined timepoints would generate state-occupancy trajectories, which would be analyzed using quasi-potential landscape reconstruction and Markov state modeling to determine whether the four-state heterogeneity observed in patient tumors spontaneously emerges in vitro, and to quantify transition rates and identify environmental drivers of plasticity. This cross-disciplinary framework transforms the organoid from a static culture model into a dynamical ecosystem amenable to the same quantitative analyses that have proven powerful in microbial community ecology.

ideas.title: Correlated multi-probe microscopy inspired single-cell multiomics integration
ideas.description: Correlative microscopy in materials science solved the challenge of obtaining multiple heterogeneous measurements from a single nanoscale region by using shared fiducial markers and precise spatial registration across modalities such as TEM, atom probe tomography, and EELS. We adapt this strategy to single-cell multiomics in glioblastoma by designing a microfluidic or combinatorial indexing platform where each individual tumor cell receives a unique tri-partite barcode that serves as a molecular fiducial, linking its transcriptomic, epigenomic, and genomic readouts. Just as correlative microscopy aligns structure-composition-property maps onto one specimen coordinate system, this approach aligns expression-chromatin accessibility-mutation profiles onto one cell identity. The platform would use split-pool ligation barcoding across three library preparation branches—RNA, ATAC, and whole-genome amplification—performed on intact nuclei before pooled sequencing, ensuring each omic layer traces back to the same cell without relying on statistical co-embedding. Applied to glioblastoma, this enables direct interrogation of how somatic mutations (e.g., EGFR amplification, IDH1 mutation) alter chromatin accessibility landscapes that in turn drive transcriptional programs of tumor heterogeneity, therapy resistance, and immune evasion at true single-cell resolution.

ideas.title: Quorum sensing-inspired framework for decoding microenvironment-driven cellular state transitions
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between bacterial quorum sensing and tumor microenvironment signaling. In microbial communities, bacteria use secreted autoinducer molecules to sense population density and coordinate collective phenotypic switches—such as biofilm formation, sporulation, or virulence activation. These transitions are governed by well-characterized bistable genetic circuits that respond to specific concentration thresholds of signaling molecules. Researchers decoded these systems by fractionating conditioned media to isolate individual signals, mapping signal-receptor pairs, building synthetic reporters, and constructing predictive mathematical models of the switch dynamics. Adapting this framework to the tumor microenvironment, we propose treating macrophage-secreted factors (cytokines, exosomes, metabolites) as functional analogues of autoinducers that drive cancer cells between four defined cellular states. The experimental approach would involve: (1) generating conditioned media from distinct macrophage polarization states and performing systematic fractionation to isolate candidate transition-driving factors, (2) deploying single-cell RNA-seq and fluorescent state-reporter cell lines to quantify state transitions in response to individual and combinatorial factor exposures, (3) identifying receptor-ligand pairs responsible for each transition using CRISPR receptor knockout screens, and (4) constructing a multistable ordinary differential equation model—inspired by quorum sensing bistable circuit models—that captures the signaling logic governing transitions between all four states. This approach moves beyond correlative observations to establish causal, mechanistic maps of microenvironment-driven state switching, enabling predictions about which combinatorial signals lock cells into therapy-resistant states and which perturbations could force transitions toward drug-sensitive phenotypes.

ideas.title: Cross-modal dynamical inference of state transition directionality with concordance validation
ideas.description: To overcome the well-documented failures of steady-state RNA velocity assumptions at complex state transitions, this approach integrates dynamical RNA velocity modeling with orthogonal temporal signals from metabolic RNA labeling and chromatin accessibility dynamics. Transition directionality between the four states is inferred independently from each modality—splicing kinetics, nascent RNA incorporation rates, and chromatin remodeling trajectories—and a concordance framework assigns confidence scores based on cross-modal agreement. Transitions supported by fewer than two independent lines of evidence are flagged as uncertain, preventing false directionality calls that plague single-modality velocity analyses. This design converts RNA velocity from a standalone inference tool into one component of a robust, falsifiable transition detection system.

ideas.title: Observer effect framework for dissociation-induced transcriptional artifacts
ideas.description: This idea draws a direct analogy between the observer effect in quantum mechanics and dissociation-induced artifacts in single-cell biology. Just as measuring a quantum system inevitably disturbs it, dissociating tissue into single cells perturbs transcriptional programs, potentially creating artificial cell states interpreted as 'plasticity.' In physics, this was resolved through weak measurement theory and quantum non-demolition techniques that minimize back-action while still extracting useful information. We adapt this by designing a multi-modal experimental framework with graded measurement invasiveness: (1) standard enzymatic dissociation (strong measurement), (2) cold-active protease dissociation (moderate measurement), (3) single-nucleus RNA-seq from frozen tissue (weak measurement), and (4) spatial transcriptomics on intact tissue sections (near non-demolition measurement). By profiling the same tissue type across all four modalities, we can apply a computational back-action correction model—inspired by weak measurement value reconstruction—to identify which cell states and plasticity signatures persist across all measurement strengths versus those that appear only under harsh dissociation. States that emerge proportionally to measurement invasiveness are classified as artifacts, while those stable across modalities represent genuine in vivo biology. This framework transforms the binary question of 'real or artifact' into a quantitative perturbation-response curve for each cell state.

ideas.title: RNA velocity as a litmus test for genuine neural lineage recapitulation in glioblastoma
ideas.description: This fused approach uses RNA velocity analysis not merely to map state transitions in glioblastoma, but as a principled test of whether GBM cellular states authentically recapitulate neural developmental trajectories or only superficially share marker expression. By computing spliced/unspliced RNA ratios across GBM states and comparing the inferred transition vectors, velocities, and branching topologies against those observed in normal neural development (neurogenesis, gliogenesis), we can assess whether GBM states traverse biologically coherent developmental paths or exhibit disordered, non-physiological dynamics inconsistent with true lineage recapitulation. States that share surface markers with neural types but display aberrant velocity fields — reversed directionality, absent absorbing states, or chaotic flow patterns — would constitute strong evidence of superficial resemblance rather than genuine developmental mimicry. This framework transforms the static debate about marker-based classification into a quantitative, dynamics-based evaluation, enabling researchers to stratify GBM states into those that are genuinely lineage-recapitulating versus those that are transcriptionally convergent through alternative regulatory mechanisms.

ideas.title: Adaptive Perturbation Screening for Cellular State Regulators Inspired by Combinatorial Materials Discovery
ideas.description: This idea transfers the high-throughput combinatorial screening paradigm from materials science to functional genomics for cellular state regulation. In materials discovery, researchers create composition-spread libraries on physical substrates and use automated, parallelized characterization to map how input perturbations (alloy compositions) determine output properties (conductivity, catalytic activity). Analogously, genome-wide CRISPR screens coupled with single-cell RNA-seq (Perturb-seq) use guide RNA barcodes as molecular positional encoders, linking each genetic knockout to its phenotypic consequence across thousands of cells simultaneously. We adapt the materials informatics concept of adaptive experimental design: after an initial genome-wide single-gene knockout screen identifies candidate state regulators, a Bayesian optimization loop—mirroring strategies used in materials search spaces—prioritizes combinatorial double- and triple-knockout libraries targeting the most informative gene interactions. Each iteration refines a predictive model of the regulatory network controlling state transitions, focusing experimental resources on uncertain or high-leverage perturbation combinations. This approach should dramatically improve the efficiency of mapping epistatic interactions among state regulators compared to exhaustive combinatorial screening, just as adaptive combinatorial materials discovery accelerates the identification of optimal compositions in vast search spaces.

ideas.title: Decomposing genetic and non-genetic drivers of cellular state via joint single-cell genomic-epigenomic lineage tracing
ideas.description: This study uses paired single-cell whole-genome sequencing and single-cell multiomics (RNA-seq + ATAC-seq) on CRISPR-barcoded isogenic clones carrying defined copy number amplifications to rigorously separate genetic from non-genetic sources of cellular state variation. By tracking lineage-resolved state frequencies within clones sharing identical CNV profiles, the design eliminates the critical confound that apparent non-genetic variation may arise from undetected subclonal copy number heterogeneity. Variance component models decompose the total phenotypic variance into contributions from copy number differences, epigenetic fluctuations, and stochastic gene expression noise, providing a quantitative hierarchy of drivers. This adversarial redesign converts a correlational observation into a causally interpretable measurement by ensuring genetic homogeneity is verified at single-cell resolution before attributing state variation to non-genetic mechanisms.

ideas.title: Wasserstein barycenters for glioblastoma state transitions inspired by economic mobility modeling
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between socioeconomic mobility modeling in economics and cellular state transitions in glioblastoma. In economics, optimal transport theory has been successfully applied to quantify how individuals move between income or occupational classes over time, treating population-level snapshots as probability distributions and computing minimum-cost transport plans between them. The Monge-Kantorovich framework yields both the optimal coupling (who transitions where) and the transport cost (how difficult transitions are), while Wasserstein barycenters interpolate unobserved intermediate distributions. We adapt this framework to glioblastoma by treating single-cell RNA-seq snapshots from different tumor stages or treatment timepoints as marginal distributions over a transcriptomic state space. Each known glioblastoma subtype (proneural, mesenchymal, classical, neural) acts as a discrete attractor state analogous to an income quintile. The ground cost metric is defined by gene expression dissimilarity (e.g., Euclidean or diffusion distance in PCA/UMAP space). Computing the optimal transport plan between timepoint distributions reveals the most likely state transition probabilities and identifies which cellular states serve as transition intermediaries. Wasserstein barycenters reconstruct the trajectory of intermediate cell populations that may not be captured in discrete experimental snapshots, potentially uncovering transient hybrid states that serve as therapeutic vulnerability windows. This approach improves on standard trajectory inference methods (e.g., RNA velocity, pseudotime) by providing a rigorous probabilistic framework grounded in distribution-level rather than single-cell-level dynamics, and by naturally handling the destruction and creation of cell populations through unbalanced optimal transport extensions.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporal dynamics of glioblastoma state transitions through longitudinal spatial transcriptomics and in situ trajectory inference
ideas.description: This study combines multi-timepoint spatial transcriptomics (e.g., MERFISH or Slide-seq) from serial biopsies or patient-derived orthotopic xenograft models with spliced/unspliced RNA ratio analysis to perform in situ RNA velocity estimation, enabling inference of state transition directionality within intact tissue architecture. By overlaying ligand-receptor interaction maps (via CellChat or COMMOT) onto spatial coordinates, the approach identifies which microenvironmental niches actively drive state plasticity versus which regions harbor stable committed states. A computational framework integrating optimal transport-based trajectory inference with spatial neighborhood graphs distinguishes between spatially determined cell states (anchored by niche signals) and stochastically plastic states (transitioning regardless of location). This design directly overcomes the static snapshot limitation by reconstructing the dynamic flow of cellular states across both space and time, revealing actionable therapeutic vulnerabilities at the niche-transition interface.

ideas.title: Phenotypic plasticity over genotype: lessons from bet-hedging in microbial ecology
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from microbial bet-hedging, where phenotypic heterogeneity in bacterial populations is driven by stochastic switching between discrete states rather than by genetic mutations. Balaban et al. showed that antibiotic persistence arises from rare, spontaneous transitions into a dormant state, independent of resistance genotype—a phenomenon formalized through bistable switching models. We adapt this framework to the cancer biology question of whether non-genetic drivers override copy number amplifications in determining cellular state frequencies. Specifically, we propose combining single-cell lineage tracing with time-resolved transcriptomics in cancer cell lines harboring well-characterized copy number alterations. By tracking state transitions at single-cell resolution over multiple generations, we can estimate switching rates between transcriptional states and compare two competing models: (1) a genetic-deterministic model where copy number predicts state occupancy, and (2) a stochastic-switching model where epigenetic or transcriptional noise governs state frequencies independently of copy number. Model selection via Bayesian information criteria or likelihood ratio tests will formally quantify the relative contribution of each mechanism. This approach directly imports the intellectual toolkit that resolved the genotype-versus-phenotype debate in microbiology and applies it to a parallel open question in cancer heterogeneity.

ideas.title: Seismic tomography-inspired inference of driver mutations from cellular state distributions
ideas.description: This idea draws a direct analogy between inferring driver mutations from cellular state distributions and seismic tomography, where Earth's internal structure is reconstructed from surface wave observations. In geophysics, the forward model predicts seismic travel times given a velocity model of the subsurface; the inverse problem recovers that velocity model from observed travel times using regularized iterative inversion with sparsity and smoothness constraints. Analogously, in cancer genomics, the forward problem maps a set of driver mutations through a gene regulatory network to produce a distribution of cellular states (observable via single-cell RNA-seq or similar assays). We propose inverting this mapping: given an observed distribution of cellular states across a tumor population, infer the most likely set of underlying driver mutations. The method would construct a differentiable surrogate of the genotype-to-state forward model (e.g., a neural ODE or variational autoencoder conditioned on mutation status), then apply adjoint-based gradient optimization over a discrete-relaxed mutation space to minimize the divergence between predicted and observed state distributions. Sparsity-inducing priors (L1 or spike-and-slab) enforce the biological constraint that few mutations are true drivers. Tikhonov-style regularization on the state-space trajectory ensures smoothness consistent with known developmental or differentiation dynamics. This tomographic inversion framework naturally handles the ill-posedness inherent in the inverse problem—multiple mutation combinations could produce similar state distributions—by providing not a single solution but a posterior distribution over candidate driver sets, quantifying uncertainty in a principled manner.

ideas.title: Optical flow methods for inferring cellular state transition directionality
ideas.description: RNA velocity infers state transition directionality by exploiting the time-lagged relationship between unspliced and spliced mRNA abundances, but it can be noisy and sensitive to model assumptions in complex multi-state systems. Drawing an analogy from computer vision, optical flow algorithms solve a strikingly similar problem: inferring motion direction and speed from static snapshots by analyzing spatial and temporal gradients. The Horn-Schunck method, in particular, imposes global smoothness constraints on the velocity field, producing coherent flow estimates even with noisy or incomplete data. We propose adapting this framework to single-cell transcriptomics by treating the gene expression embedding (e.g., UMAP or diffusion map coordinates) as an image-like field and the unspliced-to-spliced mRNA ratio as an analog of the temporal derivative. A regularized optimization, borrowing the smoothness prior from optical flow, would estimate a globally consistent velocity field across the four cellular states. This approach would be more robust to local noise than standard per-cell RNA velocity estimates and would naturally enforce coherent transition directionality across the state landscape. The method could also incorporate multi-scale analysis, analogous to coarse-to-fine optical flow pyramids, to capture both rapid local transitions and slow global differentiation trajectories.

ideas.title: Ecological Niche Engineering to Drive and Stabilize Multi-State Plasticity in Patient-Derived Organoids
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond passive observation of organoid heterogeneity to actively engineer the in vitro microenvironment to mimic key ecological niches of the tumor. A microfluidic platform houses patient-derived organoids within a tunable synthetic hydrogel, allowing for the independent application of spatial oxygen, nutrient, and cytokine gradients. By systematically varying these niche-defining parameters, the research tests specific hypotheses about the environmental drivers of each cellular state and the transitions between them. The system's output is a validated, minimal set of microenvironmental conditions required to recapitulate the four-state heterogeneity, transforming the organoid from a simple model into a causally understood, programmable experimental platform.

ideas.title: Microenvironment-conditioned optimal transport with adversarial validation for glioblastoma state transition inference
ideas.description: This approach extends optimal transport-based state transition modeling by learning a family of conditional transport maps, each parameterized by microenvironmental covariates such as hypoxia gradients, immune cell proximity, and therapeutic exposure, rather than computing a single global coupling. A mixture-of-transports framework decomposes the overall transition landscape into biologically interpretable components corresponding to distinct transition-driving programs, with component assignments inferred from spatial or single-cell niche signatures. An adversarial validation module benchmarks each inferred transport plan against available ground-truth data from lineage tracing, RNA velocity, or longitudinal sampling, automatically rejecting maps that fail to outperform null couplings. This design overcomes the fatal averaging artifact of standard OT by producing context-specific, falsifiable transition probability estimates that can be stratified by patient and microenvironmental condition.
